main_summary.summary: Richard Barry Sobol, a New York-born Columbia Law School graduate and former Arnold, Fortas & Porter associate, recounts his work as a civil rights attorney in Louisiana from 1965 to 1968 as the resident lawyer for the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC). Working alongside the New Orleans firm Collins, Douglas, and Elie (the Congress of Racial Equality's Louisiana counsel), Sobol litigated school desegregation, Title VII employment discrimination, voting rights, and fair housing cases. Centered on Bogalusa and the Crown Zellerbach paper mill, he describes the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League leadership (A.Z. Young, Robert Hicks, Gayle Jenkins), the seniority-system and Wonderlic-testing litigation that produced the 1969 Harvard Law Review article (with George Cooper) on Title VII, and his 1967 arrest in Plaquemines Parish by Leander Perez for practicing law without a license. He details the U.S. Supreme Court case Duncan v. Louisiana, voting-rights cases in Madison Parish (Zelma Wyche, Harrison Brown), the Hicks v. Weaver public-housing-siting case, welfare-fraud defense in New Orleans that anticipated Batson v. Kentucky on peremptory challenges, and the Southern Consumers Cooperative case in Lafayette. Sobol reflects on his subsequent work at the Washington Research Project (later Children's Defense Fund) with Marian Wright Edelman.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Sobol's testimony documents the operational mechanics of LCDC's permanent civil rights legal presence in Louisiana, a model that broke from short-term volunteer rotations and produced sustained federal litigation against the Crown Zellerbach paper mill's segregated job hierarchies and the seniority and aptitude-testing systems that codified pre-Title VII racial divisions. His first-hand account of Duncan v. Louisiana (the 1968 Supreme Court ruling that extended jury-trial rights to state criminal proceedings) and the federal three-judge injunction against Leander Perez's prosecution of out-of-state civil rights lawyers preserves the legal back-story of how Louisiana's parish-level segregationist machinery was disassembled.

The interview is also a rare practitioner's view of the 1969 Cooper-Sobol Harvard Law Review article on Title VII, which Sobol describes as advancing the disparate-impact reading of seniority systems that prevailed in federal appellate courts from 1967 until the Supreme Court's 1977 decision in Teamsters v. United States. His descriptions of partnerships with the Collins, Douglas, and Elie firm, with the DOJ Civil Rights Division, and with the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League (A.Z. Young, Robert Hicks, Gayle Jenkins) document the white-attorney/black-counsel/community-plaintiff collaboration that powered Louisiana Title VII enforcement.

chapters.title: New York Origins and Columbia Law
chapters.summary: Sobol describes his New York City upbringing on Manhattan's west side, his father's legal practice and mother's teaching career, his Bronx High School of Science and Union College education, and his return to Columbia Law School. He notes writing a published Law Review piece on a Supreme Court gerrymander decision in Alabama as his first civil rights exposure.
related_events: Columbia Law School Law Review publication on Alabama gerrymander decision

chapters.title: Arnold, Fortas and Porter to LCDC
chapters.summary: Sobol traces his career from clerking for Judge Paul R. Hays on the Second Circuit to joining Arnold, Fortas & Porter in 1963. He describes his initial expectation that the firm would handle Abe Fortas-style constitutional cases (referencing Gideon v. Wainwright and McCarthy Committee defendants) and his volunteer outreach to the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee through director Henry Schwarzschild, leading to his 1965 New Orleans assignment.

chapters.title: First Three Weeks in New Orleans
chapters.summary: Sobol arrives in New Orleans on August 1, 1965, expecting Selma or Jackson and instead finding sustained civil rights activity. He files a school desegregation case in St. Francisville, West Feliciana Parish, gets it summarily reversed at the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals after Judge E. Gordon West's dismissal, and decides Louisiana needs permanent lawyers rather than rotating volunteers. He partners with the Collins, Douglas, and Elie firm, attorneys to the Congress of Racial Equality.

chapters.title: Permanent Move to New Orleans
chapters.summary: Sobol returns in summer 1966 as LCDC's permanent New Orleans lawyer, brings his family from Washington, and describes Don Juneau joining the office by December and growing the staff to three or four lawyers. He sketches the Dryades Street office above the CORE storefront and his early contacts with Bob Hicks, Gayle Jenkins, and A.Z. Young of the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League.

chapters.title: New Orleans Black Bar and Bogalusa Setting
chapters.summary: Sobol profiles Robert F. Collins (later the first Black federal judge in the South), Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie of the Collins-Douglas-Elie firm, and their working relationship with the LCDC office. He describes Bogalusa's hot, paper-mill atmosphere, nightly union-hall mass meetings, the Civil Rights Act of 1964 going into force, and his first appearance in Bogalusa city court where the judge slammed a crutch on the table when Nils Douglas mentioned Abe Fortas's recent Supreme Court nomination.
related_events: Title VII Civil Rights Act takes effect July 1965

chapters.title: Crown Zellerbach and EEOC Charge
chapters.summary: Sobol describes Crown Zellerbach as the dominant Bogalusa employer with four plants (paper mill, box plant, two bag plants) and segregated unions. He details how he revised the original July 15, 1965 EEOC charge to itemize testing, seniority, segregated locals, and other practices, replacing what he viewed as the rushed original filing. He notes meeting workers at the plants and learning the substance of their complaints.

chapters.title: Bogalusa Leadership and Movement Dynamics
chapters.summary: Sobol describes Gayle Jenkins as the organizational spark who recruited A.Z. Young as president of the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League, with her cousin Robert Hicks as vice president. He notes the San Francisco support network for pressure on Crown Zellerbach, the Robert Hicks home as the center of out-of-town civil rights visitors (James Farmer, Lincoln Lynch of CORE, the (SNCC) Freedom Singers, the Free Southern Theater), and the post-1990s return litigation he handled for Black Bogalusa police officers under mayoral resistance.
related_events: Crown Zellerbach pressure from San Francisco

chapters.title: Crown Zellerbach Trial and Judge Heebe
chapters.summary: Sobol describes the consolidated trial with United States v. Local 1 in summer 1967 before Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe. He explains how his Washington antitrust-discovery experience produced a more thorough litigation pattern (interrogatories, document production, depositions before trial) than the local norm. He credits George Cooper's research on testing and DOJ Civil Rights Division attorneys Robert Moore and David Rose on seniority issues, and references Quarles v. Philip Morris and Gabrielle Kirk McDonald's role.
related_events: Crown Zellerbach Title VII trial summer 1967

chapters.title: Plant Tour, Deacons Skepticism, Travel Safety
chapters.summary: Sobol tours the Crown Zellerbach plants by court order and observes the simplicity of the jobs against the 25-year seniority gates. He describes the highest-paying Black job (palletizing the final product) and the segregated union structure across the box, bag, and paper plants. He notes his rule of never sleeping in Bogalusa or other small Louisiana civil rights towns, crossing into Natchez or Vicksburg instead. He expresses skepticism that the Deacons for Defense and Justice played the central role later historians attribute to them in Bogalusa, citing his contemporary observation and conversation with Lolis Elie.

chapters.title: Crown Zellerbach Trial Mechanics
chapters.summary: Sobol describes the eight-day Crown Zellerbach trial in which George Cooper arrived with a trunk full of testing-literature books and used them to cross-examine the company's expert witnesses. He details the chart-based presentation of seniority-impact data and Robert Hicks's continuous presence at counsel table for all eight days, which he believes affected Judge Heebe's view that the case represented a genuine community rather than lawyer-manufactured claims.

chapters.title: Wonderlic Test and Job-Relatedness Doctrine
chapters.summary: Sobol explains how Crown Zellerbach made the Wonderlic test a hiring requirement only after the Civil Rights Act passed, with no historical basis. He describes the disparate pass rates by race, the developing job-relatedness doctrine, and the cultural-bias examples (such as the RSVP question). He notes the EEOC and Department of Labor Office of Federal Contract Compliance guidelines that followed, and his subsequent Washington-area testing litigation.
related_events: Wonderlic test challenge in Crown Zellerbach litigation

chapters.title: Seniority Remedies and the Harvard Law Review Article
chapters.summary: Sobol details two seniority remedies: plant-wide seniority replacing job-progression seniority in segregated systems like Bogalusa, and random job assignment by lottery in the New Orleans Public Service Inc. (NOPSI) bus-driver case before Judge Heebe (Fagan v. NOPSI). He describes co-authoring the 1969 Harvard Law Review article with George Cooper on Title VII seniority, its wide circulation and influence in intermediate federal courts, and the 1977 Teamsters Supreme Court reversal that ended the article's prevailing-doctrine status. He notes that union-side Washington lawyers afterward held the article against him.

chapters.title: Federal Judges and Court Composition
chapters.summary: Sobol reflects that civil rights outcomes turned heavily on the disposition of individual federal judges. He contrasts the open-door welcome from district judges in New Orleans (visiting Judge Herbert W. Christenberry at home on July 4, 1967) with the dismissive treatment from Judge E. Gordon West in Baton Rouge. He notes that the Kaiser aluminum Title VII case begun by Nils Douglas took 17 years to relief, and observes that contemporary New Orleans federal courts no longer welcome the same kind of civil rights litigation.

chapters.title: Gary Duncan, Leander Perez, and Sobol's Arrest
chapters.summary: Sobol recounts representing 19-year-old Gary Duncan in Plaquemines Parish in 1967 after Duncan was charged with battery for touching a white boy's elbow during a roadside encounter. He describes Leander Perez's segregationist apparatus (with his son as district attorney), the Louisiana statutory denial of jury trial for sub-two-year offenses, Sobol's appeal to the Louisiana Supreme Court, and his arrest by Perez and a sheriff outside the judge's chambers on charges of practicing law without a license.

chapters.title: Sobol v. Perez Three-Judge Court
chapters.summary: Sobol describes the three-judge federal court (Judge Fred J. Cassibry, Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe, Judge Robert A. Ainsworth Jr.) assembled to hear the challenge to Perez's prosecution. He details the three-week trial covered daily by the New Orleans Times-Picayune, the five-party structure (Sobol and Duncan, CORE's Isaac Reynolds, federal government as plaintiff-intervenor, Perez and Louisiana, and the Louisiana State Bar Association on Perez's side), and the court's ruling that Sobol was temporarily in the state and operating with local counsel under the Louisiana statute.

chapters.title: Duncan v. Louisiana at the Supreme Court
chapters.summary: Sobol describes arguing the Duncan case before the U.S. Supreme Court, which ruled that the Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the right to jury trial against the states for non-minor crimes. After remand and a new trial with a jury, the parish retried Duncan, prompting Sobol to file Duncan v. Perez before Judge Cassibry, who enjoined the retrial as a malicious prosecution. Sobol describes Duncan's later role as a community leader and shrimper, still a friend.
related_events: Duncan v. Louisiana Supreme Court decision 1968

chapters.title: Madison Parish Voting Rights Cases
chapters.summary: Sobol describes two Madison Parish voting-rights cases under the Voting Rights Act before Judge Dawkins in Monroe. In the first, Zelma Wyche lost the police-chief race by 10 to 15 votes after the clerk of court collected approximately 200 absentee ballots from white nursing homes; the judge set aside the election under a 'discrimination in fact' doctrine. In the second, Harrison Brown's school-board candidacy was disconnected from the Democratic Party 'rooster' party-line lever; the judge ordered the election rerun and Brown won.

chapters.title: Hicks v. Weaver Public Housing Case
chapters.summary: Sobol describes Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619 (1969), brought when Robert Hicks identified Bogalusa's public-housing siting plan as placing units deep in Black neighborhoods and deep in white neighborhoods to avoid integration. The case established a private right of action under the fair housing law and resulted in an injunction halting bulldozers ready to clear the land. Sobol cites this as exemplifying Hicks's pattern-detection ability across school, employment, and housing issues including the prom-queen and football-team litigation before Judge Heebe.
related_events: Bogalusa school football and prom-queen litigation

chapters.title: Welfare Cases and Pre-Batson Peremptory-Challenge Win
chapters.summary: Sobol describes 1970s welfare-fraud defense work in New Orleans federal court with Anne Sobol and Lola C. Light, representing approximately twenty women indicted for fraudulent welfare receipt. He details the statistical challenge to the grand jury (food-stamp recipients underrepresented in jury pools) and the peremptory-challenge ruling by Judge Alvin B. Rubin in United States v. McDaniels that prosecutors could not strike all Black jurors. Sobol notes the Supreme Court adopted this rule nationally twenty years later in Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79.
related_events: United States v. McDaniels peremptory-challenge ruling

chapters.title: Chronology: Michigan, Washington, Return to Louisiana
chapters.summary: Sobol traces his career path from his August 1968 departure from full-time Louisiana work, through fall 1968 at the Institute for Policy Studies, the 1969 spring semester teaching at the University of Michigan Law School, two years at the Washington Research Project (later Children's Defense Fund) with Marian Wright Edelman, his 1971 return to Louisiana with Anne Sobol, the welfare litigation period and NOPSI bus-driver case, and his 1974 move back to Washington where he opened a law firm with Mike Trister.
related_events: Washington Research Project to Children's Defense Fund transition

chapters.title: Sweet Potato Cooperative and Loving v. Virginia Marriage License
chapters.summary: Sobol describes the Opelousas-Lafayette case in which a Black farmers' cooperative (Southern Consumers Cooperative), organized with CORE worker John Zippert and Father Albert J. McKnight, broke the white wholesaler monopoly on sweet potato sales. After local district attorney harassment and a police break-in seizing the cooperative's files, Sobol obtained federal injunctive relief. He also recounts compelling the Lafayette clerk of court to issue a marriage license to John Zippert and Carol Zippert under Loving v. Virginia.

chapters.title: Reflections on Movement Lawyering
chapters.summary: Sobol reflects that LCDC's short-term volunteer lawyers, though not cost-effective per case, were a contagion that shifted careers across the country into civil rights and pro bono work, including law students from the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council. He explains that two years in Louisiana made it impossible to return to commercial firm life at Arnold, Fortas & Porter, despite his initial insistence on a leave of absence as security, and that union-side billing clients later sustained his Washington civil rights practice.
keywords: Law Students Civil Rights Research Council
related_events: Arnold Fortas and Porter leave of absence
